text.title: Lisbon STREET FOOD Tour: 8 Snacks You Can’t Miss! 🇵🇹
text.u: I've been thinking a lot about what
[music] street food could even mean in a
city like Lisbon.
It's certainly not the chaos of Bangkok
or the late night madness [music]
and bubbling pots of Mumbai. I figured
out street food here in Portugal could
be identified [music] as the snacks
tucked inside tiny cafes, old bakeries
[music] and family run tasks. The places
where locals slip in for something
quick, cheap and comforting. A famous
place, an extremely famous place. So, we
start with a breakfast at O Trevo, a
tiny old school snack bar right in the
center of the city facing a beautiful
square. [music] It was quietly loved by
locals for decades until a certain
someone stopped by. After that, the
tourist came. But if you arrive early
enough, like I did, you still catch the
place the way Lisbon knows it. Almost
empty, just [music] a few locals and the
comforting rhythm of the morning.
Here, the thing to try is the bifana, a
simple Portuguese sandwich.
>> [music]
>> Thin slices of pork cooking and
marinating at the same time in a pan
that's [music] practically swimming in
oil. White wine, garlic, and the meat's
own juices before the meat itself gets
[music] tucked into a soft roll. Look
how beautiful it is. Bread, the juicy
slice of pork, the cheese. I use some
chili oil for this. And I'm ready to
dive in. The two slices of pork [music]
are thin, but they hold so much flavor.
Having soaked all the wine and garlic
from the bun. And once you add a little
piccante, it wakes everything up. Oh,
and a shout out to the [music] light
bun, too. It keeps the whole thing from
feeling too bread heavy. I think in this
second half of the sandwich, I'm going
to put some mustard sauce, which
actually is very typical here with the
beef.
There we are. Oh, yeah. The mustard adds
that touch of zing that you need in this
kind of sandwich. I could easily eat
another one of this. [music]
After that, pifana, I wanted something
sweet. And in Lisbon, that usually means
paste. [music]
But I didn't want just another custard
dart. I wanted the original, the real
deal. So I put on my adventurous [music]
hat and left the familiar comforting
neighborhoods of Lisbon [music] behind.
Venturing into the great unknown of the
local bus system. The kind that never
arrives when Google Maps says they
[music] will. A€ 10 round trip to the
sunny town of BM. All for a pastry that
costs a quarter of that. This better be
good. So my [music] understanding is
that every pastel denata in Lisbon
traces its roots back to this place.
[music] But technically only the ones
made here are called pastage dem. Same
family but a different closely guarded
[music] recipe. And the famous shop sits
right across from that majestic old
monastery where the recipe was actually
born. Monks, sugar, egg yolks, and a bit
of culinary alchemy that somehow [music]
survived the centuries. There we So, I
finally got my hands into this uh very
sought after type of pastry here in
Balem. They even give you um some icing,
sugar, and some cinnamon to put on top.
Got two of them. It was incredibly busy
inside. Oh, there's extremely flaky. Ah,
there we are. A famous one, pastel dem
or pastels donata. Yeah. M. It's got
milky beautiful milky aroma and it's
warm.
M really really flaky and the pasta
itself is actually lightly salty. It's
really nice. And for a change I have to
say the cream egg tart inside is not
sweet. Pretty unusual I would say for
what my experience has been so far in
Lisbon. They tend to put de leche or
some other kind of very sweetened
condensed milk or something. But no,
this one is just creamy and milky and
eggy. And
I think for the next one, I'm going to
put some cinnamon. That looks like just
the right amount.
[cough]
That was not the right amount of
cinnamon.
Portugal has a serious sweet tooth.
[music] Walk into any traditional
pastelia and you'll see it immediately.
Counters overflowing with [music] wild,
beautiful pastries in every shape and
color. And somewhere among all that
sugar and chaos, I'm [music] looking for
something specific. The Bola de Berlin,
a Berliner donut that arrived with
German Jewish refugees during World War
II. Over time, Portuguese bakers swapped
the jam for their own rich egg [music]
yolk custard. And somehow this little
donut went from outsider to a national
obsession. It's a cute little mini
sandwich. Sugary and yeah, a generous
filling of egg cream in the middle.
M. Tastes exactly like a donut. Quite
fluffy, pillowy in the inside. The sweet
egg cream on the inside. [music]
Nice. Let's go with the second one. This
is the chocolate one. Doesn't
necessarily look like Nutella. More like
a normal chocolate cream.
Actually, sorry, it's not chocolate. I
think it's salted caramel inside.
Actually, I don't mind this. The salted
caramel contrast the sweet coating
outside.
>> By the time I finished with my sweets,
Lisbon had changed moods. [music] The
sun was gone. The sky turned heavy and
gray and the air had that cool restless
feeling almost like the city [music] was
nudging me indoors telling me it was
time for something warm something with
heat. And [music] in Lisbon you can find
pretty chicken almost anywhere. But the
people who really know they know where
to go. You slip out of the postcard
version of the city and into the quieter
[music] residential alleys and tucked
between the pastel facads is this tiny
place called Frangaskeria National. The
kind of spot you'd [music] walk right
past unless you knew exactly what you
were looking for. Inside it feels like a
little island of organized chaos. A
castaway aesthetic. Crates on the walls.
[music] Wild decoration you can't quite
explain. Warm lamps throwing orange
light over everything. It's dark,
cramped, but strangely [music]
welcoming. Like stepping into someone's
secret kitchen. In the center of the
show is the woman running the entire
universe [music] behind the counter. She
has that look, the look of someone who
knows exactly how that chicken needs
[music] to taste. I've read some reviews
that say that she can be a little rude,
but she isn't. She's just a force. Part
boss, part mother, part drill surgeent
[music] of grilled perfection that's
ready to scold anyone and anything. Her
young helper works beside her with that
expression of someone who cannot under
any circumstances [music]
mess up. And if you show up unsure of
what to order, she'll remind you quickly
that hesitation isn't welcome here.
She's got chicken on the grill that
needs babysitting if it's going [music]
to be perfect. So, she wants you to do
your part. Read the menu before you get
close. It smells incredible. There is an
oily kind of spicy sauce, which is the
piri one. And then there is that green
thing that she puts on top parsley
sauce. [music]
This is something I've never seen before
in a pir chicken. And I think it's their
kind of signature thing. I would like to
kind of manhandle this chicken with my
hands, but I think I'm going to try to
be a little bit more professional at
least for the start. This piece looks
perfect. Rehydrate it a little bit into
this sauce. Oh yes.
[music]
Oh my god, the piri sauce is incredible.
Something I've never tried so far. It's
oily, natural, chili kick, a little bit
tangy. You know, both the parsley sauce
and this piri sauce keep the chicken
very juicy and moist. I'm really in
paradise right now. The parsley sauce is
creamy. Just makes the piece of chicken
creamy as well. This is just it's an
experience, a feeling that my mouth has
never had before. You really want a lot
of this piri and parsley sauce to cover
your piece of chicken.
I'm worried that this place is not as
secret as I thought it was going to be,
but this pir sauce is the spicy kick
that you get is serious. It's not like
something kind of tingling in my mouth
as well. This is crazy. I love this. I
think I'm going to go for the drumstick
next. The chicken has been seasoned with
black pepper as well. I lost the word to
describe how juicy this chicken feels in
your mouth. It's part [music] because
the chicken is juicy in itself, but also
because the oily, spicy, really the
sauce. And honestly, one of the most
beautiful thing is the chicken skin.
It's kind of crusty and almost crackly
being roasted to perfection. What an
amazing place. After you try pir chicken
here, there's no point anymore trying
chicken in the UK, in America, whatever.
Just forget it. You're gonna be really
salt.
[singing]
>> Ah, a new day in Lisbon. The city always
looks its [music] best in the morning.
The breeze coming in from the Atlantic,
the first trumps heading out, and the
wide open squares [music] glowing in
that early sunlight. I want to start
this day with something simple,
something freshly baked. Portugal has a
quiet love affair with [music] bread.
It's part of the rhythm of life here.
And if you tuck a few slices of chreso
inside [music] and chase it with a cold
beer, well, that's a serious breakfast.
I came to this place that almost look
like a church with its tall [music]
arches and long wooden tables. I like
this place because you never have to
watch the clock [music] or wonder when
the next batch is coming out. They bake
nonstop here from 9:00 in the morning
until 7:00 the following day. 22 [music]
hours of dough crafting. And the best
part, €4 for the bread and the beer.
Life looks pretty good when the simple
pleasures line up just right. Fresh from
the oven. Let's give this a try.
The bread is really fresh. It's
handmade. You can actually see it. Well,
I don't think they like you going there
and taking pictures of them doing the
bread, but And the chorizo's got this
beautiful kind of smoky taste. Cherisso
in Portugal is kind of a smoked sausage
while in Spain it's a cured sausage.
>> There's actually cheese inside. It
works. This is a beautiful thing. I wish
there was a little bit of a better ratio
[music] between chorizo and bread. Maybe
I wanted a little bit of more chorizo.
But by now you might be wondering is
Lisbon only about bread, pastries, and
grilled meat. Well, not quite. When the
sea is this close, you know, there has
to be great seafood even in the world of
street snacks. And [music] I decided to
start with something iconic, a salted
codfish crocket.
>> One little cute spot is this place. It's
called Kasa Portuguese Dupastage
Dubakalo. Hopefully I'm [music]
pronouncing it correctly. Yeah, it is a
little touristy and the whole handmade
croquette performance. Feels more like
theater than kitchen work. But it does
draw a crowd. The premium cheese they
hide inside is enough to get me curious.
But what really seals the deal is the
glass of port wine that comes with it.
Because once they tell you the price,
trust me, you'll need that [music] wine
just to process your emotions. But hey,
at least you get front row seats to some
top tier people watching in central
Lisbon. [music]
But still, I'm not really sure if I make
the best decision by putting cheese
inside cuz it's supposed to be fish
inside. So, I don't know. It does look
[music] pretty cute to me actually.
There are some greens. I think it's
parsley. And you can see the melty
cheese inside. And the rest is some kind
of starch. ch potatoes and there should
be like some mixed c [music] fish in
there. Hopefully,
that cheese inside is apparently Sarah
the Estustrala, [music] one of
Portugal's strongest cheeses. Almost as
intense as a blue cheese, but creamy and
hearty with this deep [music] funky
richness. And there's a lot of it in
here. Basically a molten ship cheese
bone with a bit of coat wrapped [music]
around it. The cheese is pretty salty.
Yeah. And there is potato and nice kind
of fresh parsley type of flavor. The
bread outside is kind of crusty. gives a
little bit of a crunch. Although, I
don't know if this cheese kind of mask a
little bit the the fish. So, I would
suggest that you kind of try both of
them with cheese and without cheese.
The next stop in this little seafood
trail is Timeout Market, Lisbon Massive
Food Hall. It's loud, busy, completely
packed. But what makes it interesting is
that the people cooking here aren't
newcomers. These stalls are run by
serious chefs, the [music] kind who
usually work in restaurants far above my
strict food budget. Here they entertain
themselves by [music] doing more casual
food, but with the same chef level
polish. One of those chefs is Marlene
Vieiraa, a chef I shamefully knew
nothing about. But several locals
pointed me her way for the best
Portuguese style [music] octopus. And
what she serves doesn't look like street
food at all. The octopus comes plated
almost like a fine dining [music] dish.
thick meaty pieces set over a soft bed
of mashed potatoes. A little bit of
spinach, olive oil, and garlic. Simple
[music] ingredients, but stunning
flavor. And the octopus itself,
unbelievably tender, delicate, clean,
balanced, the kind of touch only a
Portuguese chef seems to have with
octopus. [music] I'm really enjoying
this uh pulook. Octopus doesn't really
have that much taste on its own. It
takes the aroma or whatever kind of
sauce you you're cooking into. And this
is mostly kind of garlic and olive oil.
There's not much else to it, but it's
really nice. Just melts [music] in your
mouth. I don't know if you'll find a
better version of this recipe anywhere
else in Lisbon. I may be wrong.
I thought I was heading to a quiet
seafood spot until Google Maps walked me
straight into a full-blown party street.
Neon lights, loud music, rainbow
umbrellas, everyone gearing up for a
long night out. Not exactly my type of
place, but somewhere in the middle of
all this chaos is the little sardine
sanctuary [music] I came for. Soul and
Pesca is tiny, cramped, and absolutely
overflowing with atmosphere. People sit
shoulderto-shoulder, surrounded by
stacks of colorful things, tuna,
mackerel, sardines, [music] things you
didn't even know could be canned. The
menu reads like a catalog over the
entire Atlantic, and it's huge. I had no
idea where to start, so I just went for
three dishes. The first one is muscles
in olive oil and parsley. The broth has
this bright parsley and lemon thing
going on. Canned muscles are starchier
than fresh ones. A little firmier, a
little denser, but the flavor is light,
clean, surprisingly fresh. And we got
macareline curi sauce. This one was my
favorite. A proper spicy kick. Way more
than I expected. It's warm, aromatic,
slightly sweet. It tasted like a
Portuguese macarel's muggle into an
Asian kitchen. Then I had two nine
draws, mostly olive oil doing the
talking here with a few bits of tomato
on top. This is like very good quality
can tuna. Simple, honest, exactly what
it claims to be. And by the end, I could
already feel myself filling up all that
canned oil settling into my stomach. And
while the younger generation was just
getting their night started around me, I
knew exactly where I was heading next.
It's time for bed, Lisbon. I am done for
today.
[music]
